items.foco: Uncomplicated cystitis in a young woman
items.title: Uncomplicated cystitis in a young woman
items.contentHtml:
Uncomplicated cystitis in a young woman

  • Clinical diagnosis: dysuria, frequency, urinary urgency without fever or low back pain; urine culture is not necessary routinely unless resistance or therapeutic failure is suspected.
  • First line: fosfomycin trometamol 3 g single dose or nitrofurantoin 100 mg every 6 hours for 5 days; in regions with resistance <20% trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg every 12 hours for 3 days can be used.
  • Do not use fluoroquinolones as first line due to adverse effects and resistance; Beta-lactams are a second-line alternative.
  • Examination trap: if the patient has fever >38°C or low back pain, reclassify as pyelonephritis and change management.

items.title: Acute pyelonephritis
items.contentHtml:
Acute pyelonephritis: diagnosis and treatment

  • Diagnosis: fever >38°C, chills, unilateral low back pain, nausea/vomiting; request urine culture and blood count, with gram stain in urine to guide empirical therapy.
  • Hospitalize if there are criteria for sepsis, pregnancy, immunosuppression, structural anomaly or oral intolerance; outpatient treatment with ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours or ceftriaxone 1 g intramuscularly daily as a bridge.
  • Hospital: ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours or gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day; adjust according to antibiogram; total duration 7-14 days.
  • Do not use trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole empirically if local resistance >20%; fosfomycin is not suitable for pyelonephritis due to low tissue concentration.

items.title: Urine culture: indications and interpretation
items.contentHtml:
Urine culture: indications and interpretation

  • Indications: suspected pyelonephritis, therapeutic failure, recurrent infection (>3 episodes/year), pregnancy, men, children, immunosuppression, bladder catheter or urinary anomaly.
  • Interpretation: >10^5 colony-forming units per milliliter of a single germ is significant; in symptomatic women, >10^2 CFU/mL for Escherichia coli or >10^3 CFU/mL for others may be accepted.
  • Collection: medium stream, prior genital cleansing; if there is a urinary catheter, puncture the sampling port (not the bag).
  • Trap: urine culture with >2 germs suggests contamination; repeat the shot.

items.title: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

  • Definition: presence of >10^5 CFU/mL in urine culture without symptoms; It occurs in 2-10% of pregnant women and progresses to pyelonephritis in 20-40% if untreated.
  • Universal screening: request urine culture at the first prenatal visit (week 12-16) and repeat in the third trimester if high risk.
  • Treatment: nitrofurantoin 100 mg every 12 hours for 5-7 days (avoid in the third trimester due to risk of neonatal hemolysis); cephalexin 500 mg every 6 hours for 7 days is a safe alternative.
  • Do not use trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in the first trimester (teratogenic) nor fluoroquinolones in any trimester; After treatment, repeat control urine culture after 2 weeks.

items.title: Recurrent UTI: prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Recurrent urinary tract infection: prophylaxis

  • Definition: >3 episodes/year or >2 in 6 months; confirm with urine culture and rule out structural or functional factors (renal ultrasound, cystoscopy if persists).
  • Antibiotic prophylaxis: nitrofurantoin 50-100 mg every 24 hours or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 40/200 mg every 24 hours for 6-12 months; also fosfomycin trometamol 3 g every 10 days.
  • Non-antibiotic alternatives: D-mannose 2 g per day, cranberry with proanthocyanidins (dose 500 mg/day) and probiotics with Lactobacillus rhamnosus.
  • Trap: in women postmenopausal women, topical vaginal estrogens reduce recurrences; Do not use prophylaxis if there is an indwelling urinary catheter.

items.foco: UTI in men: causes and management
items.title: UTI in male
items.contentHtml:
UTI in men: causes and management

  • Urinary tract infection in men is always complicated until proven otherwise; causes: prostatic hypertrophy, prostatitis, lithiasis, urethral stricture, instrumentation.
  • Diagnosis: urine culture mandatory; in people over 50 years of age, digital rectal examination and prostate-specific antigen if there is suspicion of prostatitis; in young people, rule out sexually transmitted infections (Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae).
  • Empirical treatment: ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg every 12 hours for 7-14 days; if acute prostatitis, extend to 4-6 weeks with fluoroquinolones.
  • Do not use fosfomycin or nitrofurantoin in men due to poor prostatic penetration; always request renal and urinary tract ultrasound.

items.title: UTI associated with urinary catheter
items.contentHtml:
UTI associated with urinary catheter

  • Definition: urinary infection in a patient with a permanent urinary catheter or who has been catheterized in the last 48 hours; bacteriuria is universal after 30 days of catheterization.
  • Diagnosis: symptoms (fever, chills, suprapubic pain) plus urine culture with >10^3 CFU/mL; pyuria is not diagnostic on its own.
  • Management: change urinary catheter before starting antibiotics and obtaining urine culture; empirical treatment with ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours or ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours according to local resistance.
  • Do not treat asymptomatic bacteriuria in chronic catheterization (except in pregnancy or before urological surgery); remove the probe as soon as possible.

items.title: urinary sepsis
items.contentHtml:
Urinary sepsis: diagnostic criteria

  • Confirmed urinary focus (positive urine culture or pyuria) plus acute organ dysfunction evaluated with Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score ≥2 points; The quick SOFA criteria also apply (respiratory rate ≥22/min, altered mental status, systolic blood pressure ≤100 mmHg).
  • Signs of hypoperfusion: serum lactate >2 mmol/L, need for vasopressors to maintain mean arterial pressure ≥65 mmHg, or diuresis <0.5 mL/kg/hour for >2 hours.
  • Management: resuscitation with crystalloids 30 mL/kg in the first hour, ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours plus gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day as empirical therapy, control of the focus (remove tube, drain abscess).
  • Trap: do not delay antibiotics by waiting for urine culture; administer within the first hour of recognition.

items.title: Emphysematous pyelonephritis
items.contentHtml:
Emphysematous pyelonephritis: risk factors

  • Necrotizing infection of the renal parenchyma with gas production; main risk factor: diabetes mellitus (present in >90% of cases), especially poorly controlled with glycosylated hemoglobin >8%.
  • Other factors: urinary obstruction (lithiasis, tumor), immunosuppression, infection by Escherichia coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae (glucose fermenters that produce gas).
  • Diagnosis: abdominal computed tomography with gas in the parenchyma, renal pelvis or perinephric space; Huang and Tseng classification (stages I to IV) guides treatment.
  • Management: ceftriaxone plus metronidazole to cover anaerobes, percutaneous drainage or nephrectomy if unsuccessful; mortality >20% without surgical intervention.

items.foco: Antibiotics in UTI: first line and resistance
items.title: Antibiotics in UTI: first line and resistance
items.contentHtml:
Antibiotics in UTI: first line and resistance

  • Uncomplicated cystitis: fosfomycin trometamol 3 g single dose (resistance <5% overall), nitrofurantoin 100 mg every 6 hours for 5 days (resistance <10%), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg each 12 hours for 3 days only if local resistance <20%.
  • Pyelonephritis: ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours or ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours if resistance to fluoroquinolones <10%; avoid amoxicillin-clavulanic acid due to high rates of Escherichia coli resistance.
  • Emerging resistance: extended-spectrum beta-lactamase-producing enterobacteria (ESBL); if suspected, use ertapenem 1 g intravenously every 24 hours or ceftolozane-tazobactam.
  • Do not use tetracyclines or macrolides for urinary tract infection due to low urinary concentration.

items.title: UTI in children: vesicoureteral reflux
items.contentHtml:
UTI in children: vesicoureteral reflux

  • Vesicoureteral reflux predisposes to pyelonephritis and kidney damage (reflux nephropathy); suspect in children <2 years with febrile urinary tract infection, especially boys and with a family history.
  • Diagnosis: voiding cystourethrography (gold standard) to grade reflux (grades I to V); also renal scintigraphy with dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) to evaluate scars.
  • Antibiotic prophylaxis in grades III-V: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 2 mg/kg/day or nitrofurantoin 1-2 mg/kg/day until spontaneous resolution or surgical correction.
  • Trap: in grades I-II, conservative management without prophylaxis; Surgical correction is indicated if prophylaxis fails or there is renal deterioration.

items.title: Perinephric abscess
items.contentHtml:
Perinephric abscess: diagnostic imaging

  • Purulent collection in the perinephric space (between the renal capsule and Gerota's fascia); secondary to complicated pyelonephritis, lithiasis or instrumentation.
  • Imaging finding: abdominal computed tomography with contrast shows hypodense collection with peripheral enhancement (ring) and thickening of Gerota's fascia; Ultrasound reveals a hypoechoic or anechoic image with irregular borders.
  • Indications for computed tomography: persistent fever >72 hours despite antibiotics, severe low back pain, suspected gas (emphysematous) or renal failure.
  • Management: CT-guided percutaneous drainage plus intravenous antibiotics (ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours plus metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours); surgery if percutaneous drainage fails.
